description: The longfin mako is a large, fast-swimming pelagic shark closely related to the shortfin mako but distinguished by its broader, longer pectoral fins and larger eyes. It inhabits tropical and warm temperate oceanic waters globally and is far less studied than its relative. Like the shortfin mako, it faces serious pressure from bycatch and targeted fishing.
funFact: The longfin mako remains one of the least-studied large shark species — so elusive in the open ocean that a live specimen was not scientifically documented until 1966, despite the species being formally described decades earlier.
habitat: Oceanic and epipelagic waters in tropical and warm temperate seas worldwide. Tends to remain in deeper waters than the shortfin mako, typically found near the surface to depths of 220 m, often along continental shelf edges and around oceanic islands.
diet: An apex predator feeding on fast-moving bony fish — particularly tuna, billfish, and flying fish — as well as squid and cephalopods. Its larger pectoral fins and bigger eyes compared to the shortfin mako may be adaptations for slower, more deliberate hunting in deeper, darker waters.
size: Typically 2.0–3.5 m (6.6–11.5 ft); max confirmed 4.3 m (14 ft). Large specimens can exceed 200 kg, with the largest reaching ~500 kg (1,100 lbs). Females grow significantly larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Estimated Population
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — far rarer than shortfin mako; no global estimate exists

vitalSigns.label: Lifespan
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — likely similar to shortfin mako at up to ~30 years

vitalSigns.label: Reproductive Rate
vitalSigns.value: 2–8 pups per litter estimated; gestation likely ~15–18 months; long inter-birth intervals

vitalSigns.label: Age at Maturity
vitalSigns.value: Unknown — likely similar to shortfin mako; females possibly ~18+ years

vitalSigns.label: Top Speed
vitalSigns.value: ~50 km/h (~31 mph) estimated — slower than shortfin mako

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Very low — presumed similar to shortfin mako, with very late female maturity severely limiting recovery

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Enlarged pectoral fins for stability in slower deep-water cruising; larger eyes adapted for dimmer pelagic environments; regional endothermy shared with all lamnid sharks

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Tropical and warm temperate oceanic waters worldwide; Atlantic, Pacific, and Indian Oceans

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~220 m (720 ft); spends more time at depth than the shortfin mako

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: The dominant threat. Regularly caught on longlines targeting tuna and swordfish throughout the Pacific, Atlantic, and Indian Oceans. Low observer coverage means true longfin mako mortality is almost certainly severely underreported.

threats.name: Data Deficiency
threats.description: The longfin mako's rarity and deep-water habits mean its population size, stock structure, and true level of fishing mortality are almost entirely unknown — making effective conservation and management virtually impossible.

threats.name: Targeted Fishing
threats.description: Fins and meat are traded internationally. Misidentification as shortfin mako in landings and trade records obscures the actual scale of longfin mako harvest.

threats.name: Extremely Late Female Maturity
threats.description: Like the shortfin mako, female longfin makos likely mature very late (possibly 18+ years), resulting in extremely low population growth rates and slow recovery from any level of fishing mortality.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Warming and deoxygenation of deep pelagic waters may compress the thermal and oxygen habitat available to a species that depends on specific oceanic conditions for hunting and thermoregulation.

actionItems.title: Extend Mako No-Retention Rules
actionItems.description: ICCAT's 2019 North Atlantic no-retention measure for makos covers both species but is inconsistently enforced and doesn't extend to other ocean basins. Advocate for global no-retention rules at ICCAT, WCPFC, and IOTC.

actionItems.title: Support Improved Species ID in Trade
actionItems.description: Longfin mako fins and meat are routinely misidentified as shortfin mako in markets and landings data, masking true mortality. Support genetic identification programs and stricter species-level trade reporting.

actionItems.title: Advocate for CITES Appendix II Enforcement
actionItems.description: Longfin makos are listed on CITES Appendix II alongside the shortfin mako. Push for rigorous trade documentation and enforcement to prevent unreported exports from regions with high bycatch rates.

actionItems.title: Fund Pelagic Research
actionItems.description: Donate to organizations that fund satellite tagging and observer programs in deep pelagic fisheries — the only way to understand longfin mako population status, migration, and fishing mortality.

healthMetrics.label: Population Trend
healthMetrics.value: Decreasing — no region shows signs of recovery

healthMetrics.label: Habitat Quality
healthMetrics.value: Moderate — deep pelagic habitat largely intact but warming

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — tuna and billfish under heavy longline pressure globally

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Critical — high bycatch mortality; severely under-monitored

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES II; N. Atlantic no-retention (ICCAT 2019); no other basin protections

populationTrendMeta.note: Highly speculative estimates modeled from shortfin mako population trends, adjusted downward to reflect the longfin's far greater rarity. No stock assessment for this species exists. All figures carry very high uncertainty.

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Swordfish, tuna, and billfish heavily overfished throughout

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Bluefin tuna near collapse; swordfish severely depleted

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Tuna and billfish under intense longline pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Albacore and bigeye tuna overfished in key areas

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Swordfish and tuna under moderate and growing pressure

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: ICCAT no-retention (2019); decades of prior bycatch mortality

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Dense longline effort; species misidentification rampant; no enforcement

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Extensive longline fisheries; no basin-wide mako protections

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Common bycatch in Pacific tuna longlines; low observer coverage

fishingPressureRegions.name: S. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Expanding distant-water fleets with no longfin-specific protections
